banner: Week 9 · Prove It — Polygons, Quadrilaterals & Perimeter
essential_question: Can you defend the quadrilateral family tree — and explain why every quadrilateral's angles always add up to exactly 360°?
objective: Justify why every square is a rectangle (but not every rectangle is a square) and why a quadrilateral's interior angles always sum to 360°, review the week's Math-U-See practice, and pass the mastery checkpoint on classifying quadrilaterals and computing perimeter.

content.prompt: Without looking, sketch a rectangle, a rhombus, and a trapezoid from memory, and write one defining property under each. Then check your three sketches against your concept note.

content.heading: Go over Lesson Practice 8A & 8B together
content.note: Bring your completed MUS Lesson Practice 8A/8B. We check the tricky ones: the true/false questions about interior angle sums (a quadrilateral is 360°, not 180°!), whether a trapezoid must have a right angle (it doesn't), and the composite L-shaped perimeter problems where you had to find a missing side first. Fix any you missed and note WHY.

content.heading: Defend three claims
bullets: Claim 1: every square is a rectangle, but not every rectangle is a square. Argue it from the definitions — what does a square have that a rectangle doesn't require?
Claim 2: the interior angles of ANY quadrilateral always add up to 360°, no matter its shape. Argue it by splitting the quadrilateral with one diagonal into two triangles.
Claim 3: a trapezoid can never also be a parallelogram. Argue it from how many pairs of parallel sides each one is allowed to have.

content.preview: Next week, Lesson 9 — Area of parallelograms, trapezoids & triangles — where these same shapes get a second measurement.

success_criteria.text: I can classify a quadrilateral (square, rectangle, parallelogram, rhombus, trapezoid) from its properties without help
I can compute the perimeter of a simple or composite figure by finding any missing sides and adding all the sides
I can justify why every square is a rectangle (but not the reverse) and why a quadrilateral's angles always sum to 360°

questions.prompt: A quadrilateral has four right angles but its four sides are NOT all congruent. What is the BEST name for it?
questions.explain: Four right angles plus two pairs of parallel sides is the rectangle definition. It would only be a square if all four sides were also congruent.

questions.prompt: Splitting any quadrilateral with one diagonal creates two triangles. Since each triangle's angles sum to 180°, the quadrilateral's angles must sum to…
questions.explain: The diagonal splits the quadrilateral into two triangles that together cover every one of its four angles exactly once, so 180° + 180° = 360°.

A square satisfies every requirement of a rectangle (four right angles, two pairs of parallel sides) plus one extra requirement (congruent sides) — so it's always also a rectangle. The reverse is false, and a trapezoid (only one pair of parallel sides) can never be a parallelogram (two pairs).

questions.prompt: A patio's footprint is a rectangle with one rectangular corner notch removed (an L-shape), and every angle in the figure is a right angle. The overall footprint is 13 ft by 9 ft, and the notch removed from that corner is 5 ft by 4 ft. Find the perimeter of the actual patio.
questions.explain: Rubric: finds the missing sides, 13 − 5 = 8 ft and 9 − 4 = 5 ft (1 pt) + adds all six sides of the actual boundary, 13 + 5 + 5 + 4 + 8 + 9 = 44 ft (1 pt) — the same as the bounding rectangle's perimeter, 2(13 + 9), since the notch's edges are simply replaced by two new edges of equal total length.

questions.prompt: On the grid, draw and label a trapezoid — a quadrilateral with exactly one pair of parallel sides. Mark which one pair of sides is parallel.
questions.answer: Any four-sided figure with exactly one pair of clearly parallel sides marked, correctly labeled as a trapezoid.
questions.explain: Rubric: a drawn quadrilateral with one pair of sides marked parallel (1 pt) + the OTHER pair of sides clearly NOT parallel, so it doesn't accidentally become a parallelogram (1 pt).

questions.prompt: In your own words, explain why every square is a rectangle, but not every rectangle can be called a square.
questions.answer: A rectangle only requires four right angles and two pairs of parallel sides. A square meets both of those requirements AND has the extra property of four congruent sides. So every square automatically qualifies as a rectangle — but a rectangle only becomes a square if you also check that all four sides are equal, which most rectangles don't have.
questions.explain: Full credit: names that a square meets every rectangle requirement PLUS an extra one (congruent sides), which is why the relationship only runs one direction — matching the ⊂ (subset) idea from Lesson 2: squares ⊂ rectangles, not the reverse.

questions.prompt: Close: rate yourself on Lesson 8 (Emerging / Proficient / Mastered) and name the one idea you'd want to revisit.
